MK MeKi · in Elixir Contents Cheatsheet
▦ A BookBank field guide

MeKi in Elixir

A working tour of MeKi — Memory-based Expert Knowledge Injection — the architecture that scales a language model's capacity with cheap ROM storage instead of expensive compute, then a faithful implementation of it in Elixir's Nx and Axon.

from the paper by Ding, Liu, Kim, Hao, Lee, Ko & Tang (arXiv 2602.03359) · built for the BEAM

▶ Start reading ⚡ Cheatsheet

Before you turn the page

There are two ways to make a neural network smarter: give it more arithmetic, or give it more memory. For thirty years we mostly chose arithmetic. MeKi makes the other bet — and it pays off precisely where arithmetic is most expensive: the phone in your pocket.

The paper this book is built on asks one sharp question: can we grow a model's capacity using storage space, without adding any latency or FLOPs at inference? Its answer, MeKi, lets a 1.7B-parameter model rival a 4B dense model while decoding 2.26× faster on a real Qualcomm phone. We will take that idea apart, equation by equation, and then rebuild it in Elixir.

🧭
How to read this

Chapters 1–5 build the architecture from first principles (the problem, the transformer slot, the memory experts, the gate, the re-parameterization trick). Chapters 6–7 implement and serve it in Nx/Axon. Chapters 8–9 weigh the evidence and the trade-offs. A cheatsheet distills the whole thing onto one page.

cover ·

Table of Contents

  1. Storage for FLOPs Why edge LLMs are bottlenecked on memory bandwidth, and the bet MeKi makes. Why Elixir. the why
  2. The Transformer Slot A quick recap of a decoder layer, the residual stream, and exactly where MeKi plugs in. context
  3. Token-Level Memory Experts The static table Mˡ, the dynamic SwiGLU projection Gˡ, and how they fuse. retrieval
  4. Knowledge Injection The low-rank additive gate that adapts the expert vector to context, then writes it back. fusion
  5. The Re-Parameterization Trick Folding the training-time projection into one static table — the heart of zero-cost inference. the magic
  6. Building MeKi in Nx & Axon A complete custom Axon layer: parameters, embedding lookup, gate, and parallel wiring to the FFN. hands-on
  7. Re-Parameterize & Serve Fold the table after training, build the zero-FLOP inference path, and ship it with Nx.Serving. deploy
  8. Why It Works: The Evidence The benchmarks, the latency story, and the ablations — read honestly, numbers and all. results
  9. Design Notes & Trade-offs When to reach for memory scaling, how it relates to MoE/PLE/RAG, and an honest scope note. judgement
cover ·
🔑
About this book

Researched from the MeKi paper — “Memory-based Expert Knowledge Injection for Efficient LLM Scaling” (Ding et al., arXiv 2602.03359, Feb 2026) — and its stated equations and hyperparameters. The Elixir code targets Nx, Axon 0.8, and EXLA, using the documented Axon.layer/3 custom-layer API and Nx.Serving. Everything renders offline.

🧪
A note on scope

This is a faithful re-implementation guide, not a reproduction of the paper's 50-billion-token training run. The architecture, math, and code are real and runnable; the benchmark numbers quoted are the paper's own. Where we extrapolate or simplify, the text says so plainly.

Press ⌘R or reopen the book to refresh after edits.
